generations.text:
**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a banshee outside these ancient walls, and the sea roiling as if it would swallow the very earth. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than the lighthouse.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years since you left me with only silence in your wake. I have kept the light burning, as I always have, through the years of your absence. But tonight, the light seems dimmer than ever.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I will ever speak to you again. But I must try. I must write, for the wind may take my voice, and the sea may take my life, but the words must reach you.

You were my light, Eliza. The light that first drew me to this place, the light that gave me purpose when the world felt too dark. And now, as I stand alone in this tower, I fear that light has gone out—along with the warmth of your presence.

I have heard from the village that you have married well, that you have children of your own. I do not know if it is true, but I pray it is. I pray that you have found peace where I have not. I pray that you have not forgotten the man who once loved you more than the sea itself.

I have always believed in second chances

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the narrow windows of this old tower. The sea is restless tonight, and the storm that has been gathering on the horizon for days is now upon us. I have seen such storms before, and though I have learned to read the sky, I cannot say I have ever felt the sea so unrelenting, so cruel.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and I have not spoken to you in all that time. I have not written, nor have I sent a single letter. I told myself that it was for your own good, that you would not understand the weight of the words I had to say, that you would not want to hear them. But now, as I sit here, the light flickering in the darkness, I wonder if I have been the one who has been lost.

You were once my greatest joy, Eleanor. I remember the day you were born, how the sea seemed to calm itself in reverence, and the sky turned a soft, golden hue. You were the light of my life, the one who made this lonely tower feel like a home. I raised you with the same care I gave to the lighthouse, teaching you the ways of the sea, the stars, the wind, and the silence that comes between them. I taught you to read the sky, to listen to the waves, and to find strength

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the sea that surrounds me. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that makes me fear for my very soul. Yet, in this moment of chaos, I find myself thinking of you—of the daughter I once held close, and now, with bitter regret, have lost to silence and distance.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and in that time, the world has turned, and I have not. The lighthouse has stood steadfast through storms more terrible than this, but I have not stood firm in my own heart. I have not stood firm in my love for you, nor in my duty to this place—or to you.

I know the reasons for our estrangement, and I know the pain they have caused. I know the pride that led me to believe I could protect you from the world, and the fear that drove me to withdraw when you chose your own path. I was a fool, Eleanor. A blind, stubborn fool.

Tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I am reminded of the nights I once spent with you, listening to your stories by the fire, watching the stars from the lantern room, and feeling the warmth of your presence. Those nights were a balm to my soul, and now, in the solitude of this storm, I am reminded of how much I have missed them.

I do not

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I, though I fear it finds you in worse spirit. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and though I have kept the lamp burning in the tower each night, I have not kept the light of our love alive in my heart. I write to you now not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the hope that, if I do not survive the storm that approaches, you might at least know that I have not forgotten you.

The sea has been restless these past few nights, and the wind has grown louder, as though the very heavens are crying out. I have seen the sky darken with a fury I have not known in my years here. The waves rise like the hands of the damned, and the lighthouse, once a beacon of hope for those lost at sea, now feels more like a tomb. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, but I fear it may not be enough to guide the souls that drift through the night.

I have thought of you often, my dear Eleanor. I have told myself that distance is a kind of silence, and that silence is a kind of peace. But the silence of the sea is not the same as the silence of a heart that has grown cold. I have watched the moon rise and set, and I have wondered if you still look up at the same stars, or if you have turned your

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that aches with a sorrow I have long tried to bury beneath the cold, unrelenting sea. Five years have passed since I last saw you, and yet the silence between us feels heavier than the storm that now brews on the horizon. I do not know if you will read these words, or if you will even care, but I must try. I must tell you the truth—before it is too late.

The lighthouse stands tall, as it always has, but the wind tonight does not merely howl—it *screams*. The sea, which has been my constant companion for thirty years, now rages with a fury I have not seen since the night your mother died. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, but I fear it may not be enough. I fear that I may not be here to see the dawn.

You were always a child of the sea, Eleanor. You loved the salt air, the crash of the waves, the way the light danced upon the water. I remember the day you first climbed the tower, no older than seven, your small hands gripping the railing as if it were a lifeline. You looked up at me with those wide, curious eyes and said, “Daddy, can I stay here forever?” I laughed then, and I thought, *God willing, perhaps.*

But time has a cruel way of turning the tide

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The sea, as ever, has a way of whispering secrets to the shore, and tonight, it has brought with it a storm so fierce, so unrelenting, that I fear it may take more than the lighthouse. I have watched the waves rise like angry giants, crashing against the cliffs with a fury that seems almost human. I have lit the beacon, as I have done every night since you left, but tonight, the light feels different. It is not just a guide for the lost, but a beacon of my own loneliness.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, silent years. I have not heard from you, not seen your face, not felt your touch. I have tried to forget, to move on, to let the sea wash away the pain. But the sea does not forget, and neither do I.

I know you do not wish to hear from me. I know you have your own life, your own family, your own reasons for walking away. And I do not blame you. I have no right to expect you to come back, not after what was said, not after what was done. But I have spent these years wondering if I was the one who failed you, or if you were the one who gave up on us.

I have not spoken of the night you left. I have not spoken of the argument, the words

**April 12th, 1897**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the damned as the storm gathers strength outside. The sea roars with a fury that seems to echo the tumult within my heart. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the waves crash against the cliffs and the lighthouse burns with a feeble light, I find myself compelled to write.

You were always a spirited child, Eleanor. I remember the way you would run up the stairs of the lighthouse, your hair flying like a banner in the wind, your eyes alight with the fire of youth. You were my pride, my joy, my only light in the endless dark of the sea. And yet, like the storm that now threatens to consume us all, our bond has been torn asunder by tempests of our own making.

I know what you think of me. You believe I was too stern, too distant, too wrapped up in the duties of this lonely post. You left without a word, without a farewell, and I did not stop you. I told myself that it was for the best—that you needed a life beyond the rocks and the salt air, beyond the ceaseless watch of the lighthouse. But I was wrong, Eleanor. I was so very wrong.

I have spent these years watching the sea, hoping for a sign,

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these ancient walls. The sea, once my companion, now roars with a fury that makes my bones ache. I have tended this lighthouse for thirty years, and never before has the storm seemed so close, so unrelenting. I fear it may not be kind to me this time.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since you turned your back on your father, on this life, on the only home you ever knew. I have tried, Eliza, I have tried to forget the silence of your absence, the hollow spaces where your laughter once rang. But the sea remembers. It remembers your voice, your steps, the way you used to run up the spiral stairs, your small hands clutching my coat as you watched the horizon.

I have not spoken to you in five years. You sent no letters, no word. I wrote to you, but you never answered. I wondered if the sea had taken you, if the waves had swallowed your name. But now, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you again. Not with anger, but with sorrow. I have spent these years alone, save for the wind and the light, and the sea that has always been my only friend.

I have always believed in the light, Eliza. That it is our duty to guide those who sail the dark

**April 3rd, 1887**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. It has been five long, cruel years since I last saw you, and I fear the storm that now rages upon these cliffs may be the last thing I shall ever witness. Yet, as I sit here by the light, its flame flickering like the last embers of a dying fire, I find myself compelled to speak to you once more—though I know you may never read these words.

You were a child when you left, no older than ten, and I watched you go with a sorrow that has never quite left me. I do not know what you thought of me then, or what you thought of yourself. You were always a clever girl, quick to speak and quicker to leave. But I have always loved you, Eliza, even when you did not return my love.

The world has changed since you left. The lighthouse is older now, its stone worn by time and salt, and I have grown older with it. I have kept the light burning through countless storms, and though I have not seen the sea as it once was, I have come to know it well. The wind, the waves, the dark—these are my companions now, and I have learned to trust them. But tonight is different. The sky is black, the sea roars like a beast,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. I have written it with trembling hands, for I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn, or if the sea shall take me before I can say what I must.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though time has worn the edges of our quarrel, it has not softened the wounds it left. I have no excuse, no reason to justify the silence that has stretched between us like the mist that clings to the cliffs at dawn. I have been a fool, and I have been a coward. I have let the sea, the solitude, and my own pride keep me from reaching out.

But now, as the storm gathers—dark clouds rolling in from the west like a gathering army—I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years. I have spent these long nights on this lonely rock, watching the waves crash against the stone, and I have thought of the days when you would come to visit, your laughter echoing through the halls of this old keep, and your eyes full of the fire of youth.

I have not seen you in five years, Eleanor. I have not heard your voice. I have not held you in my arms. I have not even known if you were still alive. And yet, I have not forgotten you. I have not stopped thinking of you. Even now, as the wind

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old lighthouse, and the sea, that ever-faithful companion, roars with a fury I have not seen in many years. I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn, but I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have not written a single letter, and I fear that if I do not speak now, I may never speak again.

You were so young when you left—just seventeen, with eyes full of fire and a heart full of dreams. I remember the day you stood at the door, your coat flapping in the wind like a banner of defiance, and you said, “I will not be bound by this stone and sea, Father.” I did not know then that you would not return. I did not know that the world you sought would not be kind to you, and that you would not find the freedom you so desperately wished for.

I have not blamed you, Eliza. I have not cursed the sea or the stars for taking you from me. I have only mourned. And I have kept the light burning, not for the sailors, but for you. I have told myself that if I keep the light alive, so too will your memory. I have stood on the tower at dawn and watched the sun rise, and I have

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you with a hand that trembles not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken. It has been five long years since you left this house, and five years since I last heard your voice. The sea has been a faithful companion, but it has never spoken to me as you once did.

The wind howls like a banshee now, rattling the shutters and shaking the very bones of this old lighthouse. I’ve stood here for many years, watching the waves crash against the rocks below, and I’ve always known the sea was a cruel mistress. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel it is not the sea that threatens me, but the silence that has grown between us.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. Not one word. And yet, I have never stopped thinking of you. I have seen your face in the flickering light of the beacon, in the mist that curls around the cliffs, in the stars that pierce the night. You are in every breath of wind, in every whisper of the tide. I have tried to forget, but the sea never lets you go.

I know you do not wish to hear from me. I know you have your own life, your own path, and I have no right to intrude upon it. But tonight, as the storm rages and the light must be kept burning, I feel the need to say

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The sea, as ever, has been restless, and tonight it is as though the very heavens are in revolt. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that makes me fear for my life. I do not know if I shall see another dawn, and if I do, I must speak these words before they are lost to the storm and to time.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have long since ceased to hope that you would write. You were a child when I last held you close, and now you are a woman, perhaps a mother, perhaps something more. I do not know what path you have taken, nor do I care to know. The silence between us has been a wall, and I have not dared to knock.

But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you. Of the days when you would climb the steps to the lantern room, wide-eyed and full of wonder, and ask me why the light must shine so brightly. You would say, “So the ships know where to go, Papa.” I would smile and tell you that the light is not just for the ships, but for the souls who are lost—lost in the dark, lost in sorrow, lost in the silence between us.

You were my light, Eliza

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit outside these ancient stones, and the sea, that old, unyielding beast, roars with a fury I’ve never known. The waves crash against the cliff with a force that shakes the very bones of this lighthouse, and I fear I may not live to see dawn.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years since you left this place, and me, behind. I have not heard a word from you, not a single letter, not a whisper of your life. I have tried to write, but the ink would not come, and the words would not stay. Perhaps I feared what they might say. Perhaps I feared what they might mean.

You were always so clever, so bright, so full of dreams. I remember the day you left—how you stood at the door, your eyes full of fire and sorrow, and said, “You can’t understand, Father.” I tried to. I did. I thought I did. But I was wrong. And I have never forgiven myself for that.

This lighthouse has been my home, my duty, my prison. I have watched the sea for so many years, and I have learned to read its moods, its warnings. But tonight, it is different. Tonight, it is not the sea that frightens me, but the silence. The silence that has grown between

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you this evening as the wind howls like a banshee and the sea roars with fury, as if the very heavens are weeping. The lighthouse stands firm, as it always has, though my hands tremble with the cold and the weight of years unspoken. It is not the storm that troubles me most, but the silence between us—five long years of silence, and now, perhaps, the final one.

I have not spoken to you since the day you left, Eleanor. I do not know if you remember it, or if you ever truly understood why I could not keep you here. You were but a child then, and I, a man who had long since lost the will to be a father. I told you I could not give you the life you deserved, and you left in tears. I did not stop you. I did not try.

But I have thought of you every day since. I have watched the sea from this tower, and in its endless, rolling waves, I have seen your face. I have heard your laughter in the wind, and felt your absence in the hush of the night. I have kept the lighthouse not only for the light it casts upon the shore, but for the light it keeps within me—though I fear that light is fading now.

Tonight, the storm is unlike any I have seen in my years here. The sky is black as ink, and

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The sea howls outside my window, a sound that echoes the storm I have long feared—both in the night and in the silence between us. You have not written, nor have I dared to send a letter, and yet tonight, as the wind cracks like a whip against the stone walls of this lighthouse, I feel the need to speak, to be heard, if only for a moment.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since you chose to walk away from the life we once shared, from the home I built with my own hands, from the daughter I loved with all that I am. I have tried to forget, to let time smooth the edges of the pain, but the sea does not forget. It remembers every storm, every wave, every shadow cast upon the rocks below.

I have been the lighthouse keeper for many years now, and I have seen countless storms. I have guided ships through the darkest nights, through the fiercest gales, through the very edge of the world. But tonight, I do not feel so sure. The wind has grown stronger, the sea has risen, and I fear that this storm may be unlike any other. I have no doubt that it will test me, and perhaps, in its fury, it will take more than just my life.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you this evening as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with a fury I have not heard since the day you left. The lighthouse stands firm, as it always has, but I feel it tremble beneath my feet. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than the light from this tower.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years since you left this house, this life, and this father. I have not spoken to you, not written, not even thought of you. I have told myself that silence is the only way to bear the weight of your absence. But now, as I sit in the dim glow of the lamp, the sea rising in a fury I cannot control, I find myself wondering if I have been wrong.

You were a fine girl, Eleanor. Bright, spirited, with a mind that could outshine the stars. You were never meant for this life of solitude and duty. You were meant for something greater, something more. And yet, I let you go. I let you walk away, and I did not chase you, nor did I call you back.

I have spent these years watching the waves, the wind, the endless dark of the sea, and I have come to understand that the ocean is not the only thing that can swallow a man whole. The silence, the regret, the longing—these are the true tides that er

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you with a hand that trembles not from the wind, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years, my child, and yet it feels like a lifetime. I have not heard your voice, nor seen your face, since the day you left. I do not know if you remember me, or if you ever truly did. But I write, not to beg your forgiveness, for I know I have not earned it, but to say that I have been thinking of you, and I have been afraid.

The storm is coming, Eleanor. I have seen the sky darken with a fury that speaks of the sea’s wrath. The wind howls like a beast, and the waves rise higher each hour. I have stood at the top of the lighthouse, lantern in hand, and felt the very earth shake beneath me. I do not know if I will make it through the night. I have lived alone here for so long, and I have grown used to the silence. But tonight, it feels different. It feels like the end.

I have not spoken of you in these years, not because I do not think of you, but because I feared that words would not be enough. That even if I tried to explain, you would not understand. You were so young, Eleanor, and I was so certain that I was doing the right thing. That I was protecting you from the world. But I see now that

**April 12th, 1897**  
**The Lighthouse, Skerry Point**  
**Northumberland, England**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since I last set eyes upon you, and though the sea may be calm, my soul has been tossed upon the waves of regret and longing.

You were but a child when you left, and I, in my stubborn pride, allowed my anger to harden into a wall between us. I told myself that I was protecting you from the cruel world, that I was doing what a father must. But now, as I stand before the storm that threatens to consume this coast, I begin to understand the truth—what I truly did was close my heart to you, and in doing so, I lost you.

The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as if it too remembers the days when I was a man of reason, not of rage. I have spent these years watching the light, keeping the path clear for those who sail upon the dark. But tonight, the storm is unlike any I have seen. The waves rise higher, the skies darken, and I fear I may not see dawn.

**April 12th, 1887**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea, which has always been my constant companion, now roars with a fury I have not seen in many a year. The storm is coming, and I fear I may not see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five years of silence, of letters unanswered, of nights where I would have given anything to hear your voice again. You were never the same after that last argument. I remember the way your eyes darkened, the way you turned from me as if I were a stranger. I did not understand then, and I still do not.

I have spent these years tending to this lighthouse, as I always have. The light must never fail. The sea is cruel, and it does not care for the sins of men. I have seen too many ships vanish in the dark, swallowed by the waves like so many forgotten dreams. I have seen too many souls lost to the tempest, and I have vowed never to let my light go out.

You were my light once, Eliza. You were the reason I kept going, even on the darkest nights. But I suppose I was not the father you needed. I was too much a man of the sea, too much a man of

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the weight of the sea pressing in on my bones, the wind howling like a banshee in the night. The storm is coming, and I fear it may not be the sea that claims me, but the silence that has grown between us. Five years have passed since the last letter, and though I have kept the lighthouse, I have kept you at arm’s length, as though the very walls of this stone tower could shield me from the pain of your absence.

I have not spoken to you in years, and I do not know if these words will ever reach you. But tonight, as the sky darkens and the waves rise like the ghosts of my regrets, I feel the need to speak, to confess, to beg for your forgiveness.

You were a child when you left, and I was a man too stubborn to see the storm brewing in your eyes. I thought I was protecting you from the world, but I only made you a stranger to me. I told myself that the sea was too dangerous, that the world was too cruel, that I could not bear to lose you again. And so I let you go, not with love, but with fear.

I have spent these years watching the horizon, keeping the light burning for those who sail the dark waters. I have tended the flame, not for the sailors, but for you. I have whispered your name into the wind, hoping that one day

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside this crumbling tower, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth. I have not spoken to you in five long years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write, to reach out, to say what I have not dared to say before.

I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper must be steady, unwavering, a pillar of calm in the chaos of the sea. But I fear I have been a pillar of silence in my own life, a man who has let the waves of time wash away the words I once held dear. I have not written to you, nor have I called, and I know that in your eyes, I have been a ghost — a man who vanished without explanation, who left you behind like a ship abandoned on the shore.

I do not know if you will read these words, but I must say them, for I have not spoken them to anyone else. You were my light, Eleanor. You were the reason I kept the lamp burning, the reason I climbed these heights each morning, not for duty, but for you. I used to tell myself that I was keeping the light for the sailors, but I know now that I was keeping it for you — to guide you, to show you the way, to give you

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now beneath the flickering glow of the lighthouse’s lamp, its light trembling like my own hands in the cold wind that howls through the stone walls. It is a night like none other, and I fear the storm that brews beyond the horizon may not only consume the sea but also the very breath from my lungs. And yet, I write, for I have never been one to let the dark silence of the world dictate the words I choose.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and five years have been a long time to be without the sound of your voice, the sight of your face, or the warmth of your presence. I have kept the lighthouse, as I always have, not for the sea, nor for the light, but for the hope that one day, you might return. And now, with the tempest gathering strength, I find myself wondering if this is the night I shall not survive, and whether, in that moment, I shall finally be free of the weight that has pressed upon me for so long.

You remember the night you left, do you not? The storm was fierce then too, and I watched as you stood at the door, your face pale, your eyes filled with something I could not name. You said you were going to find your own way, that you could not stay here and be the daughter of a man who had never known how to love you.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the cracks of this old stone tower. The sea roars, as if the very earth is crying out, and I fear that by dawn, the light may be extinguished—not by the storm, but by my own failing strength. I do not know if I will live to see another sunrise, and if I do not, I wish for this letter to reach you, though I doubt the post will be able to carry it through the chaos.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of unanswered letters, of a heart that has grown colder with each passing day. I have tried, Eleanor, I have truly tried. I have sent you parcels, letters filled with stories of the sea, of the stars, of the constancy of the lighthouse. I have spoken of the light not as a mere beacon, but as a promise—*a promise that I will always be here, no matter what.* And yet, you have not answered.

I do not know what I did to make you turn your back on me. I thought, in the beginning, that it was the distance, the loneliness of the tower, the burden of duty. But now, as the storm gathers strength, I wonder if there was more—something I did not see, something I did not understand.

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of silence. Five years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and though the years have worn me down, they have not dulled the ache of your absence. I have not spoken to you, nor have I sent a single letter, and I know not if you would even care to read these words. But tonight, as the wind howls like a beast unleashed and the sea roars with a fury that has not been seen in decades, I find myself compelled to reach out.

You were never meant to be left behind. I know that now. I have spent these years watching the horizon, the waves, the sky, and in them I have seen the reflection of your absence. The lighthouse, once a beacon of guidance for ships lost at sea, now feels like a beacon of my own lost purpose. I have been a fool, Eleanor, to let pride and fear keep me from reaching out to you.

I have always believed that a father's duty is to protect, to provide, to stand firm. And yet, in my stubbornness, I have failed you. I have failed to be the man you deserved, the father you needed. I have allowed my own grief and guilt to cloud my judgment, and in doing so, I have lost you. I know not if you still remember me, or if the years have made you forget the man who once held you

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a banshee outside, rattling the shutters and shaking the very bones of this old lighthouse. I’ve spent a lifetime watching the sea, but tonight—tonight feels different. The sky is bruised with clouds, and the waves, they rise with a fury I have never known. I fear I may not see dawn.

You were but a child when you left this place, and I let you go. I told myself it was for your own good, that the world beyond these cliffs was a better place for you. But I was wrong, Eleanor. I was wrong to let you go. I was wrong to let the sea take you from me.

Five years have passed since you last walked these halls, since you last looked upon the sea from the lantern room. I’ve kept the light burning, as I always have, but it is not the same without you. The light is there, but the fire in my soul has long since gone out.

I’ve not spoken to you in years, and I know you’ve not spoken to me. We’ve both lived our lives in separate worlds, and I have not been the man I once was. I have been a fool, a father who failed to understand the heart of his child. I have been a man who let pride and fear keep him from reaching out.

The light must shine, no matter the storm.

**April 12th, 1894**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. The sea has always been a fickle mistress, but tonight, it feels as though it has taken on a crueler form. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that seems almost unnatural. I fear the storm may claim us all. If this letter reaches you, I pray it is in time.

Five years have passed since you left, and though I have not seen you, I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten the day you stood at the door of this lighthouse, your face pale, your eyes filled with sorrow. You said you could not stay, that you could not bear to live under the shadow of your father's mistakes. And I believed you. I believed you were right.

But I have come to wonder, in these long, lonely nights, whether I was the father you needed—or whether I was simply the one you could not forgive.

I have spent these years in quiet duty, tending to the beacon, keeping the light burning for those who sail the treacherous waters beyond. **My Dearest Eleanor,**

The lantern flickers, and I can only hope it will hold through the night. I have not seen the light of day in years, and yet tonight, the darkness feels heavier than ever.

I have not spoken your name aloud since the day you walked out, and I have not written it in a letter, not even in my own heart.
